name: Improper Inventory Management
what_it_means: Unknown, undocumented, outdated, or shadow API endpoints remain deployed and exposed without ownership, security controls, or monitoring — creating attack surface that is invisible to the security team. Attackers exploit these forgotten endpoints as they frequently lack the hardening applied to actively maintained APIs.
animated_example: Keys to old office doors still work because no one collected them when the offices were vacated — the locks were never changed and security assumes those doors are sealed.
quick_checks: Central API catalog with owner, data classification, version, and lifecycle state for every endpoint
Versioning lifecycle policy with deprecation timelines, Sunset headers, and hard-block after end-of-life
Automated reconciliation of gateway logs and runtime traffic against API inventory to surface shadow endpoints
Test, debug, and internal endpoints blocked at network layer in production — not just disabled in application config
Shadow API discovery scans run regularly using traffic analysis and active probing tools
Retired API versions return 410 Gone — not silently serving requests or redirecting to newer versions

payload_types.type: Shadow and Zombie API Discovery and Exploitation
payload_types.description: Attacker discovers and exploits API endpoints that are deployed and reachable but not in the official API inventory — including forgotten microservices, undocumented internal endpoints, or routes added during development and never removed.
examples: GET /api/internal/user-export discovered via JavaScript bundle analysis — returns all user records without authentication, not in the public API docs
Older microservice endpoint /service/legacy/admin accessible from the internet after the team forgot to update firewall rules when it was decommissioned
Development endpoint /api/dev/reset-all-passwords deployed to production through an incomplete CI/CD pipeline exclusion
Undocumented endpoint /api/v2/beta/bulk-delete found via fuzzing with common API path wordlists — no authorization check present
Shadow API discovered in mobile app network traffic that never appears in API gateway logs because it calls a backend service directly, bypassing all gateway security controls
mitigation: Maintain a live, versioned API catalog (OpenAPI/Swagger registry) with an assigned owner, data classification, environment tag, and lifecycle state for every endpoint — no undocumented endpoint should be reachable in production.
Enforce an API gateway as the single entry point for all external API traffic — reject direct access to backend services that bypasses the gateway.
Run automated API discovery tools against production infrastructure on a regular schedule to surface endpoints not in the inventory — treat any undocumented reachable endpoint as a critical finding.
Implement runtime traffic analysis that alerts when requests are received on paths not in the approved API catalog.
Require all new API endpoints to be registered in the API catalog before deployment is approved — include catalog registration as a mandatory step in the CI/CD pipeline.

payload_types.description: Attacker calls outdated or superseded API versions that remain deployed and accessible — often lacking security fixes, rate limiting, authentication improvements, or data minimization controls added in newer versions.
examples: GET /api/v1/users — v1 endpoint missing authentication controls patched in v2, returns full user list without auth
POST /api/v1/login accepting HTTP Basic Auth over HTTPS — deprecated in v2 which requires OAuth 2.0, but v1 still accepts credentials
GET /api/v1/admin/export returning full user database dump without pagination — v2 adds pagination and data minimization, but v1 remains accessible
POST /api/v1/payments/process lacking the fraud detection layer added in v2 — attacker routes fraudulent transactions through the old version
GET /api/v1/reports — v1 lacks the GDPR data-minimization filter added in v2, returning PII fields that v2 suppresses
mitigation: Enforce a strict API versioning lifecycle with communicated deprecation timelines — deprecated versions must have a public sunset date with Sunset response headers on every response.
Hard-block deprecated API versions after their end-of-life date — return 410 Gone rather than silently serving requests or redirecting to the new version.
Apply all security patches and controls retroactively to supported older versions — never maintain a version that lacks security parity with the current version.
Monitor traffic to deprecated API versions — alert on any usage and proactively contact consuming clients before forced deprecation.
Test deprecated versions with your current security test suite regularly — do not exempt older versions from security testing.

payload_types.description: Internal service-to-service API endpoints intended only for backend communication are inadvertently exposed to the internet — lacking authentication, rate limiting, or other controls assumed to be provided by network isolation.
examples: Internal gRPC service endpoint port 50051 open to the internet without authentication — intended only for service mesh communication
Admin API running on port 8080 of the same host as the public API (port 443) — firewall only restricts external access to 443, leaving 8080 exposed
Internal event bus REST API accessible from a public IP via SSRF or misconfigured routing — can inject events into internal processing pipelines
Backend API endpoint /internal/sync published to the same hostname as the public API — path-based routing misconfiguration exposes it externally
Kubernetes service with type:LoadBalancer accidentally assigned a public IP — internal cluster API accessible from the internet
mitigation: Apply network-level access controls to all internal API services — restrict inbound traffic to known internal IP ranges and service mesh identities; deny all external access regardless of path.
Run internal services on dedicated ports and hostnames with separate network security rules from public-facing services — never co-host internal and external APIs on the same exposed port.
Audit cloud and Kubernetes networking configurations regularly for services accidentally exposed to the public internet — use automated tools to detect misconfigurations.
Apply authentication (mTLS, service account tokens) to all service-to-service APIs even within the internal network — assume zero trust; never rely solely on network isolation.
Include internal endpoint exposure checks in pre-production security reviews and deployment validation — verify no internal service is reachable from the internet before every release.

payload_types.type: Test and Debug Endpoint Reachable in Production
payload_types.description: Endpoints created for development, testing, load testing, or QA purposes are not removed or blocked before production deployment — often having no authentication, full data access, or destructive capabilities.
examples: POST /api/test/createAdminUser — test helper that creates an admin account with a known password, left in production
DELETE /api/test/purge-all-data — data reset endpoint for test environments, accessible in production without authentication
GET /api/qa/bypass-auth?userId=1 — QA authentication bypass endpoint deployed to production, allows login as any user by ID
POST /api/load-test/bulk-insert — performance test bulk data endpoint with no rate limiting, usable for DoS in production
GET /api/debug/session-tokens — returns all active session tokens for all users, created for debugging authentication issues
mitigation: Use build profiles, feature flags, or conditional compilation to exclude test and debug endpoints from production builds entirely — never rely on application-level configuration alone.
Implement automated deployment validation that probes known test and debug endpoint paths and fails the deployment if any return non-404/403 responses in production.
Require code review sign-off specifically checking for test helpers, debug routes, and QA utilities before any branch is merged to the production-deployment branch.
Apply separate environment-based routing rules that block /test/, /debug/, /qa/, /dev/ path prefixes at the API gateway for production environments.
Run regular automated scans of production endpoints using common test/debug path wordlists — treat any hit as a critical finding requiring immediate remediation.

payload_types.description: Attacker uses publicly accessible API documentation, Swagger/OpenAPI specs, or JavaScript bundles to enumerate all endpoints, parameters, and internal object structures — enabling targeted attacks against undiscovered endpoints.
examples: GET /api/swagger.json in production returns the full OpenAPI spec including internal admin endpoints, management routes, and deprecated paths
JavaScript app bundle analyzed with static tools to extract all API endpoint paths hard-coded in the frontend code
GET /api/v2/openapi.yaml exposes all internal data models including sensitive fields marked internal in comments but not hidden from the spec
GraphQL introspection query { __schema { types { name, fields { name, type } } } } enumerates all available types and fields
Postman collection file committed to a public GitHub repository reveals all API endpoints, example requests, and authentication headers
mitigation: Restrict API documentation access in production to authenticated users only — or serve only a public subset of the API schema that excludes internal, admin, and deprecated endpoints.
Disable GraphQL introspection in production environments — provide schema documentation through a controlled, access-gated developer portal instead.
Audit JavaScript bundles and mobile app binaries before release to detect hardcoded internal endpoint paths that should not be exposed in client-side code.
Never commit API collections, Postman environments, or developer tooling to public source code repositories — use private repositories or secrets management for integration credentials.
Treat your API schema and documentation as sensitive information — apply the same access controls and data classification to API docs as to the API data itself.

how_to_test: Step 1: Fuzz common API path patterns (/v1/, /v2/, /legacy/, /old/, /test/, /debug/, /internal/, /admin/, /dev/, /qa/) and compare results against the official API inventory
Step 2: Analyze JavaScript bundles and mobile app binaries for hardcoded API endpoint paths — test each discovered path for accessibility and authentication requirements
Step 3: Request deprecated API versions (/api/v1/, /api/v0/) and verify they return 410 Gone or are blocked — not serving live data
Step 4: Probe framework diagnostic paths (/actuator/, /.well-known/, /metrics, /health/details) and verify all return 403 or 404 in production
Step 5: Attempt GraphQL introspection and verify it is disabled or requires admin authentication in production
Step 6: Compare runtime API gateway traffic logs against the API inventory — identify any paths receiving traffic that are not in the catalog
Step 7: Scan cloud and Kubernetes configurations for services with unexpected public IP assignments — verify no internal services are reachable from the internet
expected_outcomes: Every reachable endpoint in production is mapped to an entry in the API catalog with an assigned owner and lifecycle state
Deprecated and retired API versions return 410 Gone — not silently serving requests
Test, debug, and internal endpoints return 404 or 403 in production — not accessible from the internet
API documentation and schemas are access-controlled — not freely accessible to unauthenticated callers
Shadow API discovery scans produce no findings — all reachable endpoints are in the approved inventory
minimal_example: GET /api/v1/users on retired v1 → 410 Gone; GET /api/test/createAdminUser → 404; GET /api/swagger.json → 401 (requires authentication); runtime traffic matches API catalog 100%
mitigation: Maintain a live API catalog (OpenAPI/Swagger registry) with owner, data class, version, and lifecycle state for every endpoint — require catalog registration before any endpoint is deployed; reconcile against runtime traffic regularly
Enforce deprecation and sunset policies with Sunset response headers and hard-block or 410 Gone after end-of-life; apply security patches retroactively to all supported versions
Restrict API exposure by environment using network controls — test, debug, and internal endpoints must be unreachable in production at the network layer regardless of application config

Run automated shadow API discovery scans on a regular schedule; audit JavaScript bundles and mobile binaries for hardcoded paths; disable GraphQL introspection and restrict API documentation in production
